question.text: Which of the following is a classic example of a "merger of equals"?

answer.text: Tata Steel acquiring Corus Group in 2007
GlaxoWellcome and SmithKline Beecham forming GlaxoSmithKline in 1999

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A merger of equals occurs when both firms agree to go forward as a single new company. GlaxoWellcome and SmithKline Beecham combined in 1999 to form GlaxoSmithKline — a textbook merger of equals where both entities contributed and neither was seen as the "acquirer." **The Trap:** Tata-Corus and Vodafone-Hutchison were acquisitions (one party clearly bought the other), not mergers of equals. Facebook-WhatsApp was a straight acquisition. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series IX covers deal structures extensively. Understanding whether a transaction is a merger of equals vs. acquisition affects regulatory requirements and valuation approaches. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** In mergers of equals, watch for ego-driven disputes over management control — the "equal" label often masks power struggles that can derail the deal.
